food_subgroup: Filled and topped soft buns; enriched dough pastry

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for seaweed-floss-topped squid ink soft bun. FOODON_03315021 (bun food product) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJW covers wheat-based soft rolls and buns; used as closest parent as no specific code exists for enriched squid-ink dough buns with floss topping.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for this specific filled/topped soft bun product.

preparation_method: dressed with salad dressing and honey mustard sauce
main_ingredients: butter or margarine (implied by enriched dough)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately one bun weighing about 90 g, as typically sold at Singaporean bakeries and pastry shops.

nutrient_source.title: Bread, soft roll (enriched white flour, baked) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with component estimation for seaweed floss topping and salad dressing
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for enriched soft white bread roll (FDC ID 325871). Adjustments applied to account for squid ink dough (minor colour additive, negligible macro impact), seaweed chicken floss topping (increases protein and sodium), salad dressing and honey mustard sauce (increases fat and sugar). Final per-100g values represent a weighted composite estimate. Sodium elevated due to floss and dressing components. This is an estimated composition; exact values require laboratory analysis.

gi_source.title: White bread GI reference — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database white bread entries
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for squid-ink enriched soft bun with floss topping. GI of 70 is estimated from white bread / soft white roll reference values (GI 70–75, University of Sydney GI Database). The enriched dough base is comparable to a soft white roll. Added sugar from honey mustard sauce and salad dressing may slightly elevate effective GI. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (70 × 42.3) / 100 ≈ 29.6, using 90 g serving with 47.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from seaweed floss, salad dressing, and honey mustard sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of this bun due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g (≈378 mg per bun), which contributes meaningfully toward the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; portion control and reduced-frequency consumption are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 70) and high glycemic load per serving (~29.6) from refined wheat flour dough and added sugars in dressing and honey mustard sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat this bun as an occasional food due to its high GI of 70 and glycemic load of approximately 29.6 per bun, which can cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; pairing with protein or fibre-rich foods and limiting to half a bun may help moderate the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from enriched dough and dressing, combined with elevated sodium and added sugars
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk, this bun's combination of saturated fat (~2.8 g/100 g), sodium (~420 mg/100 g), and added sugars warrants moderation; it should not be consumed daily and should be balanced with vegetables and lean protein in the same meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Seaweed and Chicken Floss Fluffy Bun with Mustard Seed is a Singaporean-Chinese bakery pastry sold at specialty bun shops such as RUXU (入续 糖水铺与包). The bun is made using the sponge dough method, which involves a two-stage fermentation process that produces an exceptionally soft, pillowy, and slightly springy crumb. The dough is enriched with squid ink, giving it a distinctive dark grey to black colour while contributing a subtle oceanic flavour. The bun is dressed with a homemade salad dressing and honey mustard sauce, which add creaminess, mild sweetness, and a gentle tang. It is then generously topped with seaweed-seasoned chicken floss — a light, airy, savoury condiment made from shredded chicken that has been slow-cooked and dried with seaweed seasoning. Mustard seeds provide a mild peppery crunch on the surface. The overall eating experience combines a soft, moist interior with a slightly crispy, flavourful topping. Nutritionally, this bun is a moderate-to-high calorie item (~305 kcal/100 g) with meaningful protein from the chicken floss (~9.5 g/100 g), moderate fat from the dressing and enriched dough (~8.5 g/100 g), and a high carbohydrate load (~47 g/100 g) from refined wheat flour. Sodium is elevated (~420 mg/100 g) due to the floss and condiments. The glycemic index is estimated at 70 (high), making this a food that can cause rapid blood glucose elevation. It is not suitable for frequent consumption by individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. It is not halal-certified by default (pork floss variants may be used at some outlets) and contains gluten, dairy, egg, and mustard allergens.
common_user_queries: What is a seaweed chicken floss bun?
How many calories in a seaweed floss bun?
Can diabetics eat chicken floss bun?
What does chicken floss bun taste like?
Soft bun with floss topping GI

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this specific product. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy soft white roll data with component adjustments for seaweed chicken floss, squid ink, salad dressing, and honey mustard sauce. GI is estimated by analogy to white bread/soft roll reference values from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium because the base food category is well-characterised but the composite topping and dressing adjustments introduce estimation uncertainty.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (soft enriched white roll, FDC ID 325871) with upward adjustments to protein and sodium to account for seaweed chicken floss topping, and upward adjustments to fat and sugar for salad dressing and honey mustard sauce. Laboratory analysis of the actual product would be required for high-confidence values. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for this food category and cannot be reliably estimated from the composite. 3. selenium_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for this food category. 4. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on white bread/soft white roll reference values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI Values; no direct GI measurement exists for this product. 5. The food_group has been reclassified from the input value of 'Meat and alternatives' to 'Grains and staples' because the primary caloric and structural component is the wheat-flour enriched bun dough; the floss topping is a condiment-level addition. 6. Halal status is uncertain: the restaurant name and description reference 'seaweed pork floss' in the original input, but the food_name uses 'chicken floss'; both variants may be sold. The is_halal flag is set to false pending confirmation of the specific product variant. 7. Serving size of 90 g is estimated based on typical Singaporean bakery soft bun weights; actual product weight may vary. 8. All -1 sentinel values are documented above per G3 requirements.

improvement_actions_taken: Reclassified food_group from 'Meat and alternatives' (input) to 'Grains and staples' to reflect the wheat-flour bun as the primary structural and caloric component.
Established full multilingual local_names for Singaporean cuisine: Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
Derived per-100g nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy soft enriched white roll with documented component adjustments for floss, dressing, and mustard sauce.
Estimated GI at 70 by analogy to white bread/soft roll from University of Sydney GI Database; GL calculated at 29.6 per 90 g serving.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, selenium_mcg) in notes per G3 requirement.
Populated full health_context for Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and Cardiovascular Disease with specific clinical guidance.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list for perfect consistency.
Set ontology match types to only allowed values: 'closest_parent' and 'none'.
